teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the excitement of project ideas, then immediately connect that excitement to scope and feasibility.
teaching_arc.progression: Move through candidate themes, evaluation criteria, and examples of stronger versus weaker project framing.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a realistic lens for selecting and refining their final project idea.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Help students move from vague project enthusiasm to scoped, feasible industrial robotics project concepts.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand what makes a project feasible within the course timeline.
Students should be able to connect a project idea to task, risk, resources, and evaluation.
Students should leave with criteria for improving or rejecting a weak project idea.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Sound supportive, but keep feasibility standards clear.
Emphasize decisions, trade-offs, and scope boundaries.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slides.narration_seed: These ideas are designed to match your lab tools. We’re not doing real welding/painting.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students move from vague project enthusiasm to scoped, feasible industrial robotics project concepts.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Important concept (so it’s not confusing) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Final Project Ideas as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Final Project Ideas connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Final Project Ideas sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Use Important concept (so it’s not confusing) to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Our robot does material handling: pick, place, sort, load, unload
In real factories, robots often only send a signal: Tool ON / Tool OFF
So we can simulate tools (like welding) using a safe LED + simple fixture
Your grade comes from: repeatable cycle, sensors, safe logic, and clean integration
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Final Project Ideas into Important concept (so it’s not confusing) so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Roles in the system (simple) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Important concept (so it’s not confusing) to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Important concept (so it’s not confusing) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Important concept (so it’s not confusing) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Use Roles in the system (simple) to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Robot: moves parts (pick → move → place) and runs a repeatable cycle
PLC: runs the cell sequence + safety signals (start/stop, interlocks, alarms)
RPi (small computer): reads sensors, makes decisions, logs data, web dashboard (optional)
They communicate using simple signals: ON/OFF inputs and outputs (or simple network messages if allowed)
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Important concept (so it’s not confusing) into Roles in the system (simple) so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Sensor cheat sheet (plain language) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Roles in the system (simple) to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Roles in the system (simple) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Roles in the system (simple) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Define Sensor cheat sheet (plain language) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Part present sensor: “Is a part here?” (light beam sensor or proximity sensor)
Button switch: “Did the part touch the fixture?” (limit switch = small button)
Vacuum/Grip OK: “Did we actually pick the part?” (vacuum pressure switch or gripper detect)
Distance sensor: “How far / how high?” (measures a number in cm/mm)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Sensor cheat sheet (plain language) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Sensor cheat sheet (plain language) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Sensor cheat sheet (plain language) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Sensor cheat sheet (plain language) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a sensor beam and part into the scene long enough to show what is actually being detected or missed.

slides.narration_seed: Use Minimum checklist (you must show these) as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: A repeatable cycle (it can run many times with the same steps)
At least 3 sensors used in the logic (not just decoration)
A clear Emergency Stop behavior (safe stop + safe reset plan)
Students should understand what makes a project feasible within the course timeline.
Both PLC and RPi have a real job (not unused)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Minimum checklist (you must show these) to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Project menu (all fit gripper/vacuum + conveyor) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Minimum checklist (you must show these) as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Minimum checklist (you must show these) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Minimum checklist (you must show these) with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Project menu (all fit gripper/vacuum + conveyor) into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Idea 1: Conveyor sorting (red/blue or label/QR → Bin A / Bin B)
Idea 2: Conveyor inspection + reject gate (pass/fail → accept/reject lane)
Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (load → run → unload with handshake)
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
Idea 4: Kitting (build a kit box with correct items and verify)
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Project menu (all fit gripper/vacuum + conveyor) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Project menu (all fit gripper/vacuum + conveyor) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Project menu (all fit gripper/vacuum + conveyor) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Project menu (all fit gripper/vacuum + conveyor) question on their own and explain the reasoning.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slide_ref.source_title: Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong)

slides.narration_seed: Define Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Goal: Parts move on a conveyor, robot sorts them into Bin A / Bin B
Robot: pick from pickup point → move to check point → place into correct bin
Sensors (3+): part present at pickup, vacuum/grip OK, camera or color sensor (or distance sensor for size)
PLC: starts/stops conveyor, controls stack-light/alarm, handles E-stop logic
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Idea 1: Conveyor sorting cell (simple + very strong) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Define Idea 2: Inspection + reject lane (pass/fail) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Goal: Check each part and send it to ACCEPT or REJECT
Robot: pick → hold in front of sensor/camera → place to accept/reject bin
Sensors (3+): part present, vacuum/grip OK, inspection sensor (camera/height/distance/label)
PLC: runs conveyor and a simple “reject gate” output (can be a servo/solenoid or even an indicator)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Idea 2: Inspection + reject lane (pass/fail) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Idea 2: Inspection + reject lane (pass/fail) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Idea 2: Inspection + reject lane (pass/fail) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Idea 2: Inspection + reject lane (pass/fail) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Idea 2: Inspection + reject lane (pass/fail) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow)

slides.narration_seed: Define Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Goal: Robot loads a fixture, waits for a ‘machine cycle’, then unloads
Robot: pick raw part → place in fixture → signal START → wait → remove part → place in output tray
Sensors (3+): part seated switch in fixture, vacuum/grip OK, machine DONE signal (timer/button/sensor)
PLC: runs the handshake states (READY → LOADED → RUNNING → DONE) + safety interlocks
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Idea 3: Machine tending simulator (real industry workflow) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Idea 4: Kitting (build a kit box and verify)

slides.narration_seed: Keep Idea 4: Kitting (build a kit box and verify) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Goal: Fill a kit box with the correct items (A + B + C) and confirm it’s complete
Robot: pick items from different locations → place into correct slots in the kit tray
Sensors (3+): slot switches (is slot filled?), part present at pickup, vacuum/grip OK
PLC: step-by-step sequence + alarm if a step fails + E-stop behavior
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Idea 4: Kitting (build a kit box and verify) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Keep Idea 4: Kitting (build a kit box and verify) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Idea 4: Kitting (build a kit box and verify) and what to verify before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions)

slides.narration_seed: Define Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Goal: Stack parts into a grid pattern (layer 1, layer 2, …) and count them
Robot: pick from conveyor → place to pallet positions (many points) → repeat by layer
Sensors (3+): part present at pickup, vacuum/grip OK, bin/pallet present switch (optional: distance sensor for stack height)
PLC: controls conveyor + status lights + stops when pallet is full
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Idea 5: Palletizing with a pattern (great for 9+ positions) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control)

slides.narration_seed: Define Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Reality: The robot does NOT generate welding power. It only sends a signal: WELD ON / WELD OFF
So we simulate welding with: a 3D printed “weld gun” + an LED at the tool
LED ON = welding, LED OFF = not welding (same idea as real cells)
The important part is the sequence + safety + timing + logging
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Idea 6: Spot welding simulation (safe, but realistic control) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Spot welding simulation (what you actually build)

slides.narration_seed: Keep Spot welding simulation (what you actually build) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Robot: move to multiple “weld points” on a fixture (10+ points is easy)
PLC: enforces safe conditions (guard closed, E-stop OK) before allowing “weld enable”
RPi: logs weld events: point number, time, duration; shows a web page report
Sensors (3+): part present in fixture, tool at home switch, weld-point confirm (can be simple), vacuum/grip OK if handling parts
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Spot welding simulation (what you actually build) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Keep Spot welding simulation (what you actually build) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Spot welding simulation (what you actually build) and what to verify before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works

slides.narration_seed: Define Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Web dashboard: counts, cycle time, last error, pass/fail history
Calendar-based recipe: ‘Today 7–9pm run Product A’ (RPi selects a mode/recipe)
Voice command: ‘Start/Pause/Reset’ (must NOT bypass safety interlocks)
LLM helper: generate a simple recipe description (RPi), but PLC still controls safety
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Optional advanced features (RPi) — only if your base system works accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: What I expect in your demo (simple and clear)

slides.narration_seed: Teach What I expect in your demo (simple and clear) through the visual or interactive material, narrating what students should notice, why it matters, and how it connects to the topic goal.
must_cover: Show the cycle running at least 3 times with the same behavior
Explain your 3+ sensors: what each one proves (part present, picked OK, inspected OK, etc.)
Show PLC states (sequence) and show at least one fault case + recovery
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.
Show RPi output (logs or dashboard) and how it connects to the cell
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from explanation into a visual or interactive view of What I expect in your demo (simple and clear) so students can watch the concept, not just hear it.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by restating the destination of the topic: Students should finish with a realistic lens for selecting and refining their final project idea.
slides.explanation_style: Teach through the visual or interactive material on What I expect in your demo (simple and clear) by narrating what students should notice and why it matters.
slides.story_hint: Keep What I expect in your demo (simple and clear) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand what to watch for on What I expect in your demo (simple and clear) and why that visible behavior matters technically.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.
